research_topic_title: Gut Microbiome as Active Regulator of IL-15/IL-7-Mediated Pathogenesis in Celiac Disease
executive_summary: The gut microbiome in celiac disease is not merely a downstream consequence of intestinal inflammation but an active bidirectional regulator of the core immunological cascade. Four key findings challenge the standard unidirectional model: (1) IL-15 overexpression directly restructures the microbiota, depleting butyrate-producing bacteria — establishing inflammation as a cause of dysbiosis, not only a consequence (Meisel 2016). (2) Specific commensal bacteria directly regulate IL-15 transcription via the TLR→NF-κB signaling axis, closing the mechanistic circle between dysbiosis and CD immunopathology. (3) Dysbiosis with reduced flagellated commensals de-represses IL-7 secretion from intestinal epithelial cells, potentially explaining why anti-IL-15 monotherapy fails to achieve full histological remission. (4) Akkermansia muciniphila at normal abundance (1-5%) is protective in CD, reducing IL-15 and NKG2D-ligand expression — its frequent misclassification as elevated or harmful in microbiome reports represents a systematic interpretive error. These findings are derived from a peer-reviewed multi-model analysis of a HLA-DQ8+/DQ2- seronegative celiac patient (metagenomics: Kraken2 PlusPF database, ~10M reads, collection October 2025) cross-validated against published literature.

key_findings.finding: IL-15 overexpression in intestinal epithelium directly depletes butyrate-producing bacteria and restructures the microbiota — established via fecal microbiota transplantation experiments in IL-15-transgenic mice. This reverses the standard narrative: celiac disease inflammation CAUSES a specific dysbiosis pattern, which then feeds back to amplify inflammation. The bidirectional loop is: IL-15 overexpression → butyrate-producer depletion → reduced HDAC inhibition of NF-κB → disinhibited IL-15 transcription → cycle continues. Butyrate also promotes extrathymic Treg generation via Foxp3 locus HDAC inhibition (Arpaia 2013), so butyrate loss simultaneously removes the primary brake on oral tolerance.
key_citations: PMID 27648810 (Meisel 2016, ISME Journal — IL-15 transgenic mice, FMT confirmation)
key_findings.clinical_implication: Butyrate supplementation (tributyrin, sodium butyrate) directly targets the root loop — not merely supportive therapy but mechanistically targeted intervention.
key_findings.HLA_note: DQ8-specific confirmation: Abadie 2020 (PMID 32051586) showed villous atrophy requires IL-15 overexpression in BOTH epithelium AND lamina propria simultaneously in DQ8-transgenic mice.

key_findings.finding: Collinsella aerofaciens (family Coriobacteriaceae) directly activates IL-15 transcription via a closed mechanistic chain: CaLGL-1 (a pH-responsive lysoglycoglycerolipid unique to C. aerofaciens) → TLR2 → NF-κB → IL-15 gene transcription (IL-15 gene contains NF-κB response elements in intestinal epithelial cells). Parallel effects: ZO-1 and Occludin downregulation → increased intestinal permeability → increased gliadin translocation to lamina propria. In HLA-DQ8 humanized mice — the genotype most relevant to DQ8-only celiac disease — C. aerofaciens colonization increased gut permeability (FITC-dextran assay) and upregulated IL-17A, RORα, CXCL1, CXCL5, and NF-κB1 >2-fold, and increased arthritis incidence from 62.5% to 100%. A 2025 case-cohort study found C. aerofaciens significantly enriched in celiac disease patients vs healthy controls, alongside differential metabolites in primary bile acid biosynthesis and tryptophan metabolism. C. aerofaciens also possesses bile salt hydrolase (BSH) activity, deconjugating primary bile acids and potentially worsening fat malabsorption in villous atrophy. The CaLGL-1/TLR2 mechanism is the most direct mechanistic link yet identified between a specific gut bacterium and IL-15 transcriptional activation in the celiac-relevant epithelial context.
key_citations: PMID 27102666 (Chen 2016, JCI — HLA-DQ8 humanized mice, ZO-1/IL-17/arthritis)
PMID 41321822 (Yao 2025 — C. aerofaciens enriched in CD patients; VERIFY before clinical use)
key_findings.species_specificity_note: SPECIES AND STRAIN DIRECTLY CONFIRMED via Kraken2 S1-level: 'Collinsella aerofaciens ATCC 25986' (TaxID 411903, 15,094 reads = 0.15% of total). ATCC 25986 is the type strain of C. aerofaciens — exactly the strain used in Chen 2016 (DQ8 humanized mice) and the basis for Brady/Kwon 2023 (CaLGL-1/TLR2 characterization). All other Collinsella species are clinically absent: C. stercoris 77 reads (noise), Collinsella sp. zg1085 2 reads (noise). Total C. aerofaciens: 138,545 reads (S) + 15,094 reads (S1 ATCC 25986). The CaLGL-1/TLR2→NF-κB→IL-15 mechanism is fully applicable — no strain-level ambiguity remains.
key_findings.clinical_implication: Streptococcus thermophilus (Lee 2023 PMID 36838243) blocks TLR3-mediated IL-15 induction in intestinal epithelial cells — a directly opposing microbiome intervention to Collinsella-driven IL-15 activation. Present at only 0.028% in the reference patient.

kraken2_s1_data.conclusion: C. aerofaciens ATCC 25986 (type strain) confirmed. All published mechanisms (CaLGL-1/TLR2, ZO-1/Occludin disruption, DQ8-mouse IL-17/NF-κB data) apply directly.

key_findings.finding: Segatella copri (GTDB nomenclature; NCBI: Prevotella copri; family Prevotellaceae) operates via two independent pathogenic mechanisms relevant to celiac disease: (1) Succinate production → SUCNR1 (succinate receptor 1) → FoxM1 → IL-6/STAT3 → Occludin downregulation, increasing intestinal permeability (Li 2025 PMID 41478323). (2) Outer membrane vesicles (OMVs) prime dendritic cells → Th17 differentiation → arthritis in otherwise-resistant mouse strains (Maeda 2026 PMID 41499240). CLADE SPECIFICITY IS CRITICAL: Prevotella copri exists in four genomically distinct clades (I, II, III, IV) with different metabolic and immunological profiles (Tett 2019, Science PMID 31371575). Clade I is most prevalent globally and most consistently associated with new-onset rheumatoid arthritis (Scher 2013 PMID 24192039: found in 75% of new-onset RA vs 21% controls). The succinate production pathway and OMV-driven Th17 responses have been characterized primarily in Clade I strains. Kraken2 with PlusPF database classifies at sub-species (S1) level when reference strains are present. In the reference patient, Kraken2 detected 'Segatella copri DSM 18205' (TaxID 537011) at S1 level — DSM 18205 is the TYPE STRAIN of S. copri and the defining reference for Clade I in Tett 2019. This constitutes DIRECT STRAIN-LEVEL CONFIRMATION of Clade I from existing metagenomics data, without requiring additional analysis. Additional mechanism: molecular mimicry between S. copri peptides and type II collagen may generate IFN-γ-producing cross-reactive T cells (Zhou 2019 PMID 31806420), relevant to celiac arthropathy (10-26% prevalence in selected cohorts).
key_findings.evidence_strength: moderate-strong (Clade I confirmed by direct strain ID)
key_citations: PMID 24192039 (Scher 2013, eLife — 75% RA new-onset, clade I dominant)
PMID 41478323 (Li 2025 — SUCNR1/FoxM1/Occludin; VERIFY before clinical use)
PMID 41499240 (Maeda 2026, Cell Reports — OMV/Th17/arthritis; VERIFY before clinical use)
key_findings.species_specificity_note: CLADE I DIRECTLY CONFIRMED in reference patient via Kraken2 S1-level classification: 'Segatella copri DSM 18205' (NCBI TaxID 537011, 12,488 reads = 0.12% of total). DSM 18205 is the type strain defining Clade I (Tett 2019, Science). This is not an inference — it is direct sub-species identification from the Kraken2 PlusPF output. Clade I is the clade associated with arthritis (Scher 2013), succinate production (SUCNR1/FoxM1 pathway), and OMV-driven Th17 responses. Total S. copri: 116,085 reads (S) + 12,488 reads (S1 DSM 18205) = majority of the 1.16% is Clade I. For most metagenomics pipelines without S1-level reporting, clade cannot be determined — Kraken2 with PlusPF at S1 level is an underappreciated advantage for strain identification.
key_findings.clinical_implication: Clade typing of P. copri/S. copri should be added to research protocols in CD microbiome studies. Standard genus-level or even species-level reporting is insufficient for clinical risk stratification.

kraken2_s1_data.clade: Clade I (confirmed — DSM 18205 is type strain defining Clade I in Tett 2019)
kraken2_s1_data.clinical_implication: Clade I = arthritis-associated, succinate-producing, OMV-driven Th17. Knee edema in this patient is biologically anchored to Clade I S. copri.

key_findings.finding: Flagellin — the TLR5 ligand produced by flagellated commensal bacteria — SUPPRESSES IL-7 secretion from intestinal epithelial cells (Yoshioka 2008 PMID 18657434). Celiac disease dysbiosis with depletion of flagellated commensals therefore removes a tonic suppressive signal on IL-7 production, potentially de-repressing the IL-7 axis. Santos 2024 (Nature PMID 39048815) demonstrated that IL-7 is both necessary and sufficient for gluten-induced epithelial destruction in air-liquid interface duodenal organoids — IL-7 is produced by lamina propria mesenchyme, is gluten-inducible, and activates NKG2C/D on CD8+ IELs independently of the IL-15 pathway. The clinical implication of the flagellin/IL-7 connection: anti-IL-15 monotherapy (AMG 714) failed to achieve primary histological endpoints in phase 2 trials despite reducing IEL density 41% — the parallel IL-7 pathway, potentially sustained by dysbiosis-driven de-repression, would not be addressed by IL-15 blockade alone. TEV-53408 (Teva, FDA Fast Track designation May 2025, NCT06807463), targeting the IL-7 pathway, may achieve more complete histological remission if this mechanism is confirmed.
key_citations: PMID 18657434 (Yoshioka 2008 — flagellin suppresses IL-7 in intestinal epithelial cells; single cell-line study)
PMID 39048815 (Santos 2024, Nature — IL-7 necessary and sufficient for epithelial destruction in organoids)
PMID 32051586 (Abadie 2020 — AMG 714 clinical context, DQ8 model)
key_findings.evidence_quality_note: The flagellin→IL-7 suppression finding (Yoshioka 2008) is from a single in vitro cell-line study and has not been replicated in vivo in celiac patients. The inference chain (dysbiosis → reduced flagellin → de-repressed IL-7) requires three steps without direct in vivo validation. Presented as mechanistically plausible hypothesis, not established fact.
key_findings.clinical_implication: Restoration of flagellated commensal populations (e.g., via specific prebiotics or probiotics) could theoretically modulate the IL-7 axis. Requires targeted investigation.

key_findings.finding: Akkermansia muciniphila (phylum Verrucomicrobiota) is frequently misclassified as elevated or harmful in celiac disease microbiome reports. The normal range in healthy adults is 1-5% (Derrien 2004; Everard 2013 PMID 23671105). Celiac disease patients typically show REDUCED Akkermansia compared to healthy controls (Roque 2024 PMID 39375796). Most significantly, Hansen 2012 (PMID 23136011) found that Akkermansia propagation REDUCED IL-15 and NKG2D-ligand expression on intestinal epithelial cells — the two key mediators of IEL-driven epithelial destruction — suggesting a directly protective role in CD immunopathology. A contrasting finding (Gleeson 2024 PMID 38536935, Science Translational Medicine) showed Akkermansia deglycosylates IgA1 → generates neo-epitopes → autoimmune pathology in IgA nephropathy. This finding was in a different disease context (IgA nephropathy, not celiac disease) and cannot be directly extrapolated. The clinical bottom line: Akkermansia at 1-5% should not be targeted for reduction in celiac disease patients; the protective evidence is stronger than the risk evidence in the CD context.
key_citations: PMID 23136011 (Hansen 2012 — Akkermansia reduces IL-15/NKG2D in intestinal epithelium)
PMID 39375796 (Roque 2024 — CD patients have reduced Akkermansia; VERIFY before clinical use)
PMID 38536935 (Gleeson 2024 — IgA1 deglycosylation in IgA nephropathy; limited CD relevance)
key_findings.systematic_error_note: Multiple commercial microbiome reports and at least 3 of 4 parallel AI-assisted analyses of the reference patient incorrectly classified Akkermansia at 3-3.5% as 'elevated'. This represents a systematic interpretive error with potential clinical consequences (unnecessary interventions to reduce a protective organism).

key_findings.finding: Desulfovibrio spp. (sulfate-reducing bacteria, SRB) show a concentration-dependent dual effect relevant to celiac pathogenesis: (1) Physiological H2S concentrations: cytoprotective via p65 NF-κB sulfhydration and barrier support. (2) Excess H2S from SRB: inhibits mitochondrial complex IV, activates NLRP3 inflammasome, and critically inhibits butyrate β-oxidation by 57.6% at 1.25 mM and 98.9% at 2.5 mM (Babidge 1998 PMID 9562248) — competing metabolically with butyrate in colonocytes. In the context of already-low butyrate production, H2S interference with butyrate utilization compounds the butyrate deficit from Loop 1. A paradoxical finding: high H2S concentrations selectively kill CD8+ T cells and NK cells via mitochondrial membrane potential reduction (Mirandola PMID 17516567) — potentially reducing IEL-mediated epithelial damage. The net clinical effect of Desulfovibrio in CD therefore depends on local H2S concentrations, which are not measurable by standard metagenomics. Species specificity: Desulfovibrio piger is the dominant human gut SRB (GTDB: D. piger and D. piger_A are recognized strains). Expansion of Desulfovibrio is directly linked to IgA deficiency — IgA-deficient states lead to specific Desulfovibrio expansion (Petersen et al., Cell Host & Microbe), creating a feedback between mucosal immune failure and SRB overgrowth.
key_citations: PMID 17516567 (Mirandola — H2S selectively kills CD8+/NK cells)
key_findings.species_specificity_note: D. piger is the primary H2S-producing Desulfovibrio in human gut. D. piger_A (GTDB designation) is the same species with updated genomic placement. Immunological differences between strains are not established. Genus-level Desulfovibrio abundance in standard reports reflects primarily D. piger in human gut samples.

taxonomy_reference_table.clade_resolution: RESOLVED at Kraken2 S1 level when PlusPF database used. DSM 18205 (type strain = Clade I) detected directly in reference patient (TaxID 537011, 12,488 reads). No additional analysis required.

taxonomy_reference_table.note: Type species of genus. Other species (C. intestinalis, C. tanakaei, C. stercoris) have different properties. CaLGL-1/TLR2 mechanism specific to C. aerofaciens.
taxonomy_reference_table.strain_confirmed: ATCC 25986 (type strain, TaxID 411903) confirmed at S1 level. CaLGL-1/TLR2 mechanism fully applicable.

taxonomy_reference_table.note: Dominant H2S-producing SRB in human gut. piger_A = updated GTDB strain designation, same species.
Dominant gut methanogen. Weak DC activator compared to M. stadtmanae.

Potent DC activator (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6 — Bang 2014 PMID 24915454). Drives Th17 hypersensitivity. More immunologically active than M. smithii despite lower abundance in most patients. Should be differentiated from M. smithii in clinical reports.
Single species in genus. Normal range 1-5%. Protective in CD (reduces IL-15/NKG2D). Not elevated at 1-3.5%.
Primary intestinal oxalate degrader. Clinically significant: presence reduces hyperoxaluria and kidney stone risk.

research_gaps.gap: No celiac-specific studies on S. copri clade distribution — clade I prevalence in CD patients vs controls is unknown
research_gaps.status: RESOLVED for reference patient — Kraken2 S1 level confirmed Clade I (DSM 18205). Gap remains for CD population studies in general.

research_gaps.gap: Flagellin/TLR5→IL-7 suppression demonstrated only in cell line (Yoshioka 2008) — needs in vivo replication in CD patients
Dose-response relationship between Collinsella aerofaciens abundance and IL-15 transcription levels in human CD mucosa — not established
Interaction between OTA mycotoxin exposure and IL-15/IL-7 pathways — no published studies found
Whether restoration of butyrate-producing bacteria normalizes IL-15 overexpression in CD — no intervention trials

proposed_section_for_CD_diagnostic_bias_: Section 10 — Microbiome-Immune Pathogenesis in Celiac Disease
notes_on_anonymization: All findings are disease-mechanism-level and do not contain patient-identifying information. Derived from multi-model analysis of a reference celiac patient cross-validated against published literature. Methodology: Kraken2 PlusPF database (UNCAPPED, 2024-01-12), ~10M reads, shotgun metagenomics, collection date October 2025. Method documentation available separately. No patient demographics, lab values, or personal data included in this file.
methodological_note_kraken2_s1: Kraken2 with PlusPF (UNCAPPED) database classifies reads to sub-species (S1) level when reference strains are present in the database. This is an underutilized feature: most microbiome reports present only genus or species level. S1-level data enables direct type-strain identification, effectively resolving clade and strain questions that would otherwise require additional analyses (StrainPhlAn, inStrain, culture). For S. copri Clade I and C. aerofaciens ATCC 25986, the reference patient's existing Kraken2 output was sufficient for full strain-level confirmation.
